ayoreo:
Icatecãcoi iji yurigode enga icatecã́ri enga ojninga: “Dupade, átajape yurigode aja yejnaréngone aja yu,” chiese ojninga. “Uásejna yejnaréngone. Yacaranguipise uje diyajmánguipíse uje ipésu badode to rĩ, diyajmángapo yejnaréngone rĩ,” chiese ojninga. Chojninga:
“Yacaranguipise. E iraja baruode. Gome,” chiese chojninga.
english: And then as*Ichajúidé*came nearer, Jesus called his name: “¡*Ichajúidé*á!”:

ayoreo: Ore pésu gu uje, yocajñamicho gu uje cucha oméjna ome yoque.
english: They play it because it’s a fun game to play, a sort of celebration for when things are going well.

ayoreo: Mu yujnusínango to gajiné uje chi deréjnai tudé chi yocajnúone u anírone, yocajnúi tu Noé. Jesus cho quique enga chojninga: “Noé urásade ore cha tangári Noé acajnaquéode nanique. Enga a ore cha yu to jne rĩ enga a ore cho cucha bisidéguiji yuruode. A ore chicadígui jeta yiríringai tu. A ore cha yu enga a ca ore changureta yu go.”
english: But there’s more he told. This is what they say he said: “My arrival will only happen when all the Cojñone and the Tribal people have had a chance to believe in me; when, from your mouths, they have heard my words.”

ayoreo:
Uje Dupade chi chojnirame ore
diríca: “Ca acho jne. Acho cuchade jnese iji yejnai tuné, mu ca acho poria
tuaté adie jne.”
english: For this is what God had said to them: “Don’t eat from it. You can eat everything else in my garden, but don’t eat the fruit from that tree.”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “A cuchape yabi ¡jõ!”
english: But*Guébi*said nothing. He knew exactly where the water had come from and how he’d gotten such a lot of it, but he didn’t tell his father.

ayoreo:
David gotique a u uje chi
chojninga: “Yucue jórogui yiquicho erãpe. Yucué, yucué ujetiga qué yacose yu
ome Dupade, ga yicai jnumi eruéode. Yicai yote quedéjna ajéode, mu Dupade
déji.”*(Salmo 139:1 aja 7 ore)*
english: You know, He is the all-knowing one, and we don’t know anything.

ayoreo:
Mu*Ujai*potarása
ica ga chicáji*conferencia*to. Que
pota to iji ejode udojo mu a déji to jne. Déji dínique to jeta pota Dupade Uruode
to. E pota to gu. A irasé to jne. E pota iglésia gu jetiga e chise ajei.
Chetaque ica mu e pota to.
english: But then,*Ujai*would want to go to meetings with me. But lately he hasn’t been going. One day he’ll want Dupade’s Word also. At least he’s open to it and likes it now. He used to be very opposed to it.

ayoreo:
Chiese*Ichajúidé*catecãrápoi to rĩ, mu que
yiraja*Ichajúidé*uruode. (Ore cãra
darúo. Jec ore cãra daruode.) E gusu yuruode, Señorítabia. Ta jé yuricho diríca. Qué déji yu mu jé cho yojñane uje uásejna.
english: And*Ichajúidé*now went toward him and Jesus called to him: “Come. Come. Come.” This is what Jesus said to*Ichajúidé*.

ayoreo: *Idaidé*– Étacõre – Ducodegose
english: –*Picanerãi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode (*People of the River)

ayoreo:
Jé basája ga a yabi David a
jnosi. Chicási tõniñeque enga toi, enga chicai punire quedéjnai. Uje yijócãre
uje angoroneparigape. Mu que ayorérique déji siñeque ujeta chojninga: “Bape
garãte u que, que etógapo ome ua gotique rĩ. Cucherãi unejángome ua.”
english: It’s the same with any one of you. He might stop believing in*Dupade*, but his state of being*Dupade*’s believer does not leave him at all, because it is intact in heaven. I could go far away. We might remove ourselves from our family, but the clan names we were given long ago would remain our clan names. I would still be their son. I would still be their child.

ayoreo:
Mu Jesus
chojninga: “A yingo yiríringai ome uhuaque gu uje yigaidi tu jeta jec ũrépise
uágome uacanguretarétigai jeta yiyague uacamánique,” chojninga.
english: But Jesus said: “I’m telling you the truth: I am coming back. I want you to be sure that your faith is sincere so that when I suddenly appear, you will be ready.”

ayoreo:
Chi ore
chucué ore, mu que ore chise ore go. Ore chise, anírone, oidode*foto*carubode iji jnumone. Enga ore
chiraja gu udojo uje chi ore chejna ore, mu ore chijóchame ore. U ayorepísode
ore chijóchame degode ore uje ore pógu ore iji ore idai uté.
english: Little by little the news of the killing of the missionaries reached other groups of us Ayoré.

ayoreo:
Jecute ore chojninga: “Jé go, a Dupade
u,” ore chojninga.
english: Adam said: “Did*Dupade*lie to us? Someone’s coming, it’s probably*Dupade*! Why, it is*Dupade*!”

ayoreo:
Mu Jesuse chi chojninga: “Cachi
jiei á. Uacamóño to iji diríca uje qué yiji*perdonar*ome uaque diríca,” chojninga. “Enga uaque gajnui jé u ome uaque. Chorase
yáyipieraque gajiné,” chi Jesus chósi tome dacadigode. Chi íjnoque deréjnai iji
dirome, yijorane uaque, iji durase udaque. Mu beyoi ga anguretáyo Dupade jé iji
de diri. Ca yãrango yoc.
english: Time is running out, my friends, even this morning. Come now, and really believe in God today. Let’s don’t deceive ourselves.

ayoreo:
Mu
chojningame yu: “Atata gotodie ayoredie.” Mu chiese
ojninga: “Que yatata ore uje idája ore iji yu. Jé cachi ca araja ore íode.”
english: And*Ichajúidé*said: “I know you, you are*Dupade*’*s*Son, you are Jesus.”

ayoreo: *Jadia – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: *Jadia – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: Yujátadé:                    Cutamurajane ore eduguéjna bajade uña.
english: *Yujátadé:  He was of the Cutamurajane Clan and was one of their first leaders.

ayoreo: (Catecã́ri Caratai)
english: *Acacomi Yui Caratac*

ayoreo: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 11 de octubre de 1985.*
english: *Cajoide – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*

ayoreo: Yirajágo,*Dupade*, uje yocumajosṍri déji uje Satanas u, enga Satanas u*dicore*,*Dupade*. Satanas chetaque baruode. Que pota gotique,*Dupade*, ujetiga ayoréode yirajágo baruode, mu yirajágo uje Satanase sue yoquibai uje ujopié cuse uje sue yoquetotiguei gai,*Dupade*. Sue yocáyipie ayoré gai, mu yocacaranguipise,*Dupade*, uje yocangureni tu babi Jesuse,*Dupade*. Yocangureni tu babi Cristo,*Dupade*, enga babi Cristo chingome yoque uje uté déji yocari,*Dupade*. Enga uté pota ujetiga chátaja yoque,*Dupade*, ujetiga chisi ome pirajtigai tome yoque jeta yirajágo baruode. Jé upo rĩ,*Dupade*, uje chirote yoque, ujetiga aniri, uté chãraque yogome Satanase.
english: We know,*Dupade*, that we have an enemy who is*Satan*, and that*Satan*is a walking spirit.*Satan*hates your Word. He doesn’t want us to know your Words, and we know that*Satan*is more powerful than we are, and his strength is greater than ours. He is smarter than we humans are, but how thankful we are that we have your Son*Jesus*and he is*the one we listen to*, and he is much greater than our enemy*Satan*. We trust in your*Son Christ Jesus*and your Son tells us that he is with us. He tells us he wants to help us and give us understanding of your Words. He also takes care of us and protects us from*Satan*.

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Campamento Ayoré, Bolivia – 1971**Faith Wyma churúsãre Ecarai atatagode udojo nanique*
english: Two Ayoré men could be hunting honey in the woods when Jesus comes back again; one of them has faith in*Dupade*, but the other one doesn’t. One would go up into heaven, and the other who is left behind will wonder what happened to his friend. He couldn’t accompany his friend to heaven, because he hadn’t put his faith in*Dupade*. But the other one did believe, and he went to heaven.  We Ayoré, here on earth, stick together -we go on trips together and go hunting, and hang out together, but afterwards we all return to camp. But when*Dupade*comes to take his believers, he might find us on a hunting trip together in the woods and take only the person that believed in Him. And that is why*Dupade*talks about how all of us on earth should believe in Him today.

ayoreo: “Utácai udore u anire, uje ore jno Dupade Uruode údi aja erámi jnenga ore chacate Dupade Uruode ome ore iji erámi. Enga chojninga: “Mu chugúperone u Satanás anírone utocaidode*demónionie*. Satanas u uje chi doi Dupade Uruode iji ayoréode ajéode, cojñone ajéode enga tagu. Que tagu mu chetaque ome ore, enga chetaque ore ome Dupade Uruode. Enga Dupade Uruode u semilla enga gajnui unojo u ujeta chugúperone tagu Dupade uruode iji ore ajéode.”
english: Jesus went on explaining the parable and said: “You will notice that, in the parable that I gave, a farmer sowed seeds and some of them fell into the weeds, and as the weeds grew, they overcame the plants that came up and they all died. This is like the person that hears the*Word**s*of*Dupade*, but his mind is always on worldly things and so it is that he completely forgets my*Word*.”

ayoreo:
Jé ueradipise yoquihermána Rosadaté acajnaquéode. Uje que chudute cuchárique diríca, Dupade’s Uruode jé u gu. Ga nína darodie yui uje ore diringai u. Irasique darodie yui. Ga a gajnui u Dupade dirome ujetiga di. Ga queyirasique Dupade yui jetiga di dirome. Que yimóngo jetiga di gajneri, jeoga dirome u. Gajnui u uje Dupade irasique dabidie yui. Ga di dirome ga ore irasique Dai Dupade diringai yui. Jé uacamóño uje Dupade ipesute yoque. Ipesute queyibijnani ujetiga yirarã́co. Ipesute queyangoninguinone jetiga yangácoi cuchade. Mu que yimo uje yajire Dupade
Uruode iji cojñone uruode que. Mu chamáñaje yajire*poquito**versículo*iji
anire, queyuruode jne. Quĩrique jé cho udore Rósadé ore ojningáiso. Ore
chojninga ujetiga ajnórañopise ñane. Ga oyo parácho iji jnumi ome ñane. Enga
angáchopísi ñane quitígo. Quitígo chojmára ome ñane rĩ. Ca angáchoi
cucháringome uaque. Ca oyo cucha daté iji uaque rĩ. Gusu jetiga ajnórañopise
ñane. Angáchopísi uacojode ayoré. ¿Socaseti irajataque gosi ome uaque jne a? Dupade ajéode u ujetiga queyáyipiedie chojmára iji jnumi iji de, iji jnumi jóroque.rep Jetiga yidotágo yógome cuchade. Mu chamáñaje chequedíe jé arajáyo anire to gu uje déji Santiago. Mu que yiraja versículo. Josuédé a chiraja. Mu que yiraja jogadi gu uje ga que yatatame uaque. Mu Dupade urúo. Chojninga: “Adotáyo uágaja ome cucha.”*(Santiago 4:1-6)*E gusu udojo yuru*poquito*. Uerate Bai versículodie uje chajire Dupade Uruode qué. Yocuruode. Uje chojninga: “Ca oyo cuchadaté iji uaque.” (Romanos 12:3) Dupade chetaque.  Uje udúco Pablo uruode to uje chojninga: “Ca
oyo cuchadaté iji yu,” chi chojninga Pablo. “Que yipota jetiga ayorérique
chojninga, ‘Irajataque Pablo. Irajataque ga chiraja cuchade jnese’; que
yipota,” chi chojninga Pablo, Apostol Pablo.
english: What our sister Rosadaté taught us is very good. She hadn’t heard anything yet, because of staying home to read Dupade’s words. She was happy about the fact that her daughters were coming. She was excited to to see them. It was as though she was about to see Dupade. We welcome Dupade’s coming when we will see him arrive in the future. We don’t know if he will come today or in the future.  Just like Rosedaté being happy to see her daughters, Dupade will be happy to see his daughters when he comes. And when he arrives, his daughters will be happy to see their Father Dupade, also. You know that it was Dupade who made us – he made our mouths so we could talk. And he made our ears to we could hear things. I only read Dupade’s words in the words of the Cojñone. I can only read very few verses in our own language. It is a little like what Rósadé and them have said. They said we should love each other. And be kind to each other. And to really listen to each other. And try to agree and think alike. And not to listen to gossip about each other. Not to think we are better than others. And to only love each other sincerely. Ready to listen well to our fellow-Ayoréos. Isn’t it possible that someone else might know more than you do? It’s Dupade’s will that we think alike and honor each other even though we are in this world. We should be patient with each other. But you women know what it says in the book of James. I’m not sure of where that verse is. ***Josuédé might know. I can’t remember where it’s found so I can’t tell you where it is. But it’s in Dupade words. It says: “Be patient.” (James 4:1-6) These are just a few words of mine. The verses that Bai read to us from Dupade’s words were very good. And he read them in our own language. When he said: “Don’t think too highly of yourselves.” (Romans 12:3) Dupade hates that. We hear the Apostle Paul’s words about himself. He says: “Don’t think too highly of me. I don’t want anyone to say, ‘Paul is really smart. He knows everything.’ I don’t want you to say things like that about me.” This is what Paul the Apostle said about himself. That is how we should be. We shouldn’t think we’re better than others. Dupade hates that kind of thinking. We should admit that we don’t know everything.

ayoreo:
Ore poté tudaque cucha a u uje ore chiraja uje
choquíbi ore; ore chiraja pajegátigade.
english: But*Dupade*was truthful, and in one sense so was Satan, for if we wouldn’t have eaten that fruit then we wouldn’t have been ashamed. We’d be happy with each other. We wouldn’t get anger. We wouldn’t have been embarrassed of being naked. But we did eat it, and Satan found a way to enter us and we learned to sin.

ayoreo:
Ore chisa jóroque
dabade iji yote dequejnagóri jmainie. Enga a jé adi tu ujetiga Dupade di iji
jnumi te ga. A yocuñéngo, yocurasade uñéngo, cojñone a chisa jóroque dabáyode
iji Dupade ujuyaquei jmainie ujetiga cho dajuñame jnumi. Noé urasade
unojo, chi ore chuñieta nanique ome ipesudi cuco. Chi ore chojninga: “¡Chimo
jóroque gotique! uje Noé pésu cucoi. ¿Ja guei icasirase? Cachi ipesute cucoi.” Mu que ore
chajire gaté uje Dupade chiroque iroi Noé aja cucoi ujetiga pésu. Noé chingo
darasade ujetiga ore cha date gajnaréngone enga Dupade ca pésu cucha
gajnarétique ome ore. Dupade ca cho dajuñame ore jetiga chimesẽre ore atígo.
Mu jno gaté gojni Noé uje chi chingo Dupade Uruode aja ore enga chi chojninga:
“Ayáyo uacate gajnaréngone,” chi chojninga. “Chi Dupade cho dajuñame yoque jeta
ca yiyágo yocate gajnaréngone.” Mu doi datéringane uje ore chujñieta gaté. Mu gajnui jec u diri tuné gajnerĩ uje cojñone iji erámone, ayoréode iji erámone, uje ore chujñieta Dupade Uruode. Ore chojningame Dupade Uruode: “Cachi Dupade Uruode atatasórone ore ojña.” Mu que cachi yocojña gotique. Jec ũré yoque jecute jnani bajade unojo ore chimo jóronga dínique uje Dupade cho dajuñame ore enga quene ore chucué Dupade ujetiga changureta. Mu e que qué dínique dépoi gotique ujetiga Dupade qué chuchẽrape cucoi gai enga ore chacapója rĩ. Enga adi jec
u uje ayoréode, cojñone a ore pota jóronga ore nóna yoque to enga a ore
chojninga: “¡Jesute a, ayape ga yijína uaque to jne!” Mu Dupade
Uruode que qui u go. Dupade Uruode qui u uje chojninga: “Qué yijna yirógode ore
pijnani uje cama yi ore údi iji jnumi.” Enga jec ũré ujetiga ayorérique chucué
yocacajnaquéode iji Dupade adode enga doi ga changureta Dupade uje cama Dupade
cho dajuñame jnumi.*(2 Pedro 2:5)* Yijóchago
uacajéode údi ujetiga ajnusínaño Juan Bautista uru quigode uje chojninga
nanique: “Écãcho uacajéode.” Juan Bautista uté chimo Jesus uje chose ore
yui nanique enga chojningame darijogosórone: “Cajicho to jnacari uté. Uté u jne
ujetiga toi ome erámone jogosórone yoquisijnaringuéone. Enga gajnua u áyasa
perané ome Dupade. Enga Dupade jé guerani u dajnacari uté ome yoque.” Jecute
cuchayi tupúsu Dupade uruode uje chojningame yoque: “Angáchoi yujnacari, ga
jé ca yujuseque uáguiji jnumi.”   E gusu. *Ecarai – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1960**Ecarai chajnerame don Jaime Dunn uruode nanique**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai uruode udojo*
english: The people of Noah’s time made fun of the boat he was making. They said, “Of all things! Noah is making a boat. Has it even rained? He’s making the boat for nothing.” But they didn’t observe the sky when*Dupade*sent his servant Noah into the boat that he made. Noah had told his countrymen to stop doing ugly things, for if they would stop their ugly ways then*Dupade*would not bring trouble upon them.*Dupade*would not punish them if the things they did were acceptable to him. But things went ahead as planned for Noah and he kept telling them*Dupade**’s Words*and saying to them: “Stop your ugly ways,” he said. “If you don’t,*Dupade*is going to punish us, if we don’t stop what we’re doing.” **today****,****both****Cojñone****and****A****yoréode****mak****e****fun of****Dupade****’s Words:** It’s even worse how they are making fun of*Dupade**now*. Just like back then, the*Cojñone*in the world, and the*A**yoréos*in the world, they’re all laughing about*Dupade’s Words.*They say things about it: “The preachers of*Dupade’s**Words*are just making it up.” But they aren’t just making it up. We’re telling the truth, and the men today are just like the those who lived long ago, and only when*Dupade*starts punishing them will they start looking for*Dupade*and believing in him. But there will be no more time left for*Dupade*to open the boat and let them in! And just like it was then, the*Ayoréos*and the*Cojñone*they will all want in vain to go with us, and they will say: “Jesus, wait for me, I want to go with you!” But that isn’t in*D**upade**’s Word*at all!*Dupade**’s Word*says: “I’m speaking through the lips of my servants while there is still time to believe before I come back.” And it’s true that right now a person can still look for our teachings about*Dupade**’s ways*and put his faith in*Dupade*before*Dupade*comes to punish the earth.*(2 Pe**ter**2:5)* So I beg you today to remember the words of John the Baptist when he said: “Change the way you think.” John the Baptist saw Jesus and said to those around him: “Look at that young man over there. He is the one who will die for the sins of people all over the earth. He is like the lamb our fathers sacrificed to*Dupade*for their sins. And*Dupade*himself is giving his Son to die for us.” Therefore, let’s especially let*Dupade**’s Words*speak to us which say: “Listen to my Son, and I will treat you as well as I treat him.” That’s all. Dupade – God Yoque Dupade – Our God After children – Descendants (lit. disi ejode) God’s Words Teller – Prophet Jaime – Refers to Hershel Dunn who contacted the Ayoréos near Rincón de Tigre Dupade’s Son – God’s Son Dupade’s Words – God’s Word, Bible Cojñone – People not Ayoré Ayoreos – Ayoré people He fell – He was born Sárode – Type of native medicine that includes chanting and blowing

ayoreo:
¿Gotique gu a
uje cojñone churúsãre darusarane ome dachidedie piesórone cojñoc? Ore
churúsarame ore íode tiji darujna aone jeta ore chiraja ga jé ũré ga ore doi
oré tome. Ga Dupade churúsãre yoquíode iji darujna aone to. Chidójninga jeta
jec ũré jeta yanguretágo ga yoqui déji darujna aoi pucuecaringuei gajnéone iji
gatájnoquei. Ga Dupade chidójninga udojo.
english: Therefore, if someone wants to accompany his relatives, or a wife wants to go with her husband, or a husband wants to go with his wife, tell*Dupade. Dupade*will forgive your sins, because his Son Jesus paid the debt you owe for your sins by dying for you on the cross.

ayoreo: Jé noña ga cabayui oidi ayoré noñome cuchiso iji toniñeque.
english: They say the tapir said: “Your words, what were they?”

ayoreo: Chiraja gu ga ore chucué jne. Jĩrique pógu re. Cheru carujnangue iji ti. Chi chingo raja dajõranie. Chojninga: “Uñec a chise yu jne, a yabai jne iji yiyangóri. A yeru carujnangue ga yimo iji gatei jne.”
english: Everyone was getting tired and*Corabé*said: “I’m going to go to sleep now.”

ayoreo: Ore i uñai u:**Cutérepaogóronegosóde**
english: *Chabotodiegosode –*“People of the bats.”

ayoreo: Ga*cucujna*chojninga: “A yijína yajábi ga yipatíorango ñane ome gotique.”
english: *The gopher said: “I’ll stay with my dear brother the jaguar and whatever that thing does will happen to us both.”*

ayoreo: Jnuruminone – pajei carubode
english: They would shake their heads and make their tied-back hair move back and forth. They would paint themselves with the powder that came off the red stone called*carodé*. The strings on their sandals were also painted red.

ayoreo: Uje yocáquiode ore chatatapo uñeque rĩ uje chi ayoré dacasutedie gajnenie jé u. Chopise ayoré dacasutedie poti jnu ãri adode rĩ. Uje chi ipesudé ayoré dacasutedie nanique jnu ãri.
english: (Oidábiadé said: I would like to make this comment, our forefathers did not yet know long ago about the influence of Satan’s demons, or that Satan is the author of killing.)

ayoreo: *Cajoidé –  Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1985.*
english: The medicine man has the power to curse as well as heal. When a medicine man mistreats or curses people and causes them to die, however, the people kill him for he has become a danger to his people.

ayoreo:
Dupade
iroque, Dupade anguresṍre Tíquico, ga que doi ga cutame Dupade atique. Ga jé
ũrepise uje yicagoi udojo to ica, Rincóngosode ore, enga icai ome Dupade
Uruode. Mu adi jec u uje cho jeta yoquidáyo, ga iyajmaque yóguiji mu
yacaranguipis ome Dupade uje Dupade Uruode que chijnime re iji Rincóngosode.
Que chijnime re iji Zapocogosode go. Que chijnime re iji Tobitégosode.
english: What Paul taught was true. He said: “I sent Tychicus to you so that he could tell you how things are going with us.”

ayoreo: Yingome ore garósiode to ica,*Dupade Uruode*iji ta, mu que ore changureta yu to gotique. Ore chotigase. Ga jnanione uñane chojninga: “Chamo jóroque gotique uje ore chácari Tobité iji yiquei uté. Que cuchárique tṍraji yiquéode,” ore chojninga.
english: I’ll tell you a little about a donkey that burst out and talked to his owner. But in the beginning the donkey’s owner doubted his donkey and was angry with him. But the donkey could see*Dupade*’s angel in front of them and went in another direction. But the owner couldn’t see the angel. His mind was on the things he had to do. You’ve probably heard something about this when they’ve taught you.

ayoreo: Mu*Jupi*chojninga: “Be*Iyai*gari ga amóji.”
english: But I left*Orégajnúi*because he was jealous of*Jupi*, my former husband.

ayoreo: *Guébi*chojninga: “Yapade a ore di jne,” mu que ore todo. Ore josipise ayorepísiji dayé. Dayé u guéode. Que ojningai u ayoré go, isasṍri. Chojninga: “Ca acayóji yiguíjnai. Sijnac uágome yu gu.”
english: Finally, the boy returned, and some time later the father went off to hunt with the other men. He didn’t think of taking the boy, however, and he left him in the camp.

ayoreo:
Mu
yicatecãcoi Dupade jétiga Dupade chátaja daruode aja yoque jeta yirajágo
quigode uje Dupade Uruode jé qui jé u.*(Santiago
1:5)*
english: It’s always good when our Ayoré countrymen arrive. Or when Ayoréos from other places visit our group. When believers come they give us their testimonies and tell us what*Dupade*did for them wherever they went. Not only do we like to hear their news, but*Dupade*loves to hear it too.*(Malachi 3:16)*

ayoreo: Ga ore doi ga ore chasẽra chugúpẽredatéi ga jiei ore ga ore chasẽra jnese. Ga chi chóna abuei. Chijna iyoi. Chi gajneque iyojna. Ga jé noñája abuei ajei. Gati ajei.
english: And when he stopped at the lake,*Diti*shot his arrows and killed him. He shot him and drug him off. And that is how he finished him off.

ayoreo: *Picanerane*ore erane u*Tarajane, Umarajane*ore ga ore tujua dáringane deyácho.
english: Two of the **Picanerãi Clan*tunes were **Tarajane*and**Umarajane*, and they would announce them when they sang.

ayoreo: Yijñecarósi iji de. Chiese*Ipeyai*nero yu nanic. Gajnei marínga yipógu yachachagúgode quitic ome*Uchiedie*ejoi.
english: *Tajerogue Chijñecare*

ayoreo:
Mu Jesuse que uruode u gotique.
Chojninga: “Piotiguéode qué u uje yiroque yirógode ore aja erámi,” chojninga.
“Ujetiga ore chingo ore jne uje a yo yujuñame erámi te jne,” chojninga. Jé uacamóño uje jnani bajade,
Jesuse chiroque ore uje ore chojningame*proféta*.*Proféta*udore u uje ore chatata Dupade
Uruode bajaque aja ore iquéode. Chirógaja ore nanique, mu que ore tangári
anírone gotique, ore acajnaquéode. Ga uyoque yocajnúone jé u*profétade*ore uje yingongo Dupade
Uruode aja ore, yocujode ayoréo iji ne jnumi. Enga ujetiga apota ajína yoque to
jnenga, ango Dupade ujeta chĩro basijnaringuéone to. Enga na dajóningai taja
ua to. Na pajei sereringai taja ua to. Na pijnoraquei taja ua to ujetiga apota
ajína yoque to aja gatajnoquei.
english: Your earthly body wants to do those things, for it always wants to be like the world. But I thank*Dupade*, that*Dupade*loves us, and that he sent his son Jesus to this world. And then, when his son Jesus went back to heaven, he sent another to earth, and he will be with us forever.

ayoreo:
Chi uñeque chojninga to: “José á,
arajape yurigode to,” chojninga. “Que yiraja gotique que uje yiji enga yiji
durasede enga, José, yiji durasede iji yurigode enga yijnoque yibosode iji
canásta iji yatoi durasede. Mu chugúpẽre chagúode iji gatajnoque tagu yibosode
iji yatoi durase,” chojningame José. Mu José e chojninga: “Iraja. Enga
imóringo u uje ujetiga barósõri tuté irógode chujé ua jne enga ore tṍra ua
aja ajãro enga chugúpẽre chagúode tagu ua iji guesi.”
english: The great king said to him: “I was unhappy about my dreams. The*daijnane*could not explain them to me, because they didn’t know what they meant.” Then Pharaoh said: “I’m going to get rid of those*daijnane*all over the earth, because they couldn’t tell the meaning of my dreams.”

ayoreo: Ga arei ga yapúcoja jogadi uje yirajágo uje que yotique déji erámi ga yijóchago chicórone ga yimáco gu chicórone.
english: The “*Iyatade”*who prepare things for the Festival also paint their bodies black with carbon. They do it in different ways.

ayoreo:
Jecute
yirajágo uje ayoréode yirajágopo rĩ uje que qué cuchárique bisideque iji
erámi. Ga yirajágo to uje gusu Jesus uje chosõre yógome, anire,
yoquejnaréngone. A pota ga chiqueta yoque. Je pota ga chujé yoque. Ga yirajágo
uje udore*cinco misionérone*toi iji ayoréode jmainie, mu Dupade e chiraja
ore cachodi uje cáma tocade.
english: It’s told that we Ayoré used to want chiefs that would kill our*cojñone*enemies. But many of the*cojñone*wanted to make peace with the Ayoré, but we did not know this.  The Ayoré chiefs also wanted to be praised for their generosity, for their compassion on the sick as they would blow on them to heal them. But the*cojñone*and the Ayoreos thought differently and this added to their misunderstandings.

ayoreo:
Ga
chojningasia: “A jec ũré,  mu a Dupade
ajéo ga jnorase yúdi, jeoga a ca jnorase yúdi jeta jé ca ajéo.”
english: The*cojñone*were always talking with us when we were working at the lumber mill. One of the workers said to me: “My father and his family are believers.”

ayoreo:
Que yetétique cuse go iji
Dupade. Yirasiguipise. Chamáñaje e yicatecã́ri gu.*(Santiago
4:8)*
english: There is nothing I don’t like about Dupade. I love him. That is because I now talk to him.

ayoreo:
Dupade, átaja yujodie chequei,
uyoque to gajneri, ga uneripise baruode yui ome yoque gajneri, yoque Dupade.
Yirajágo uje jiei yoque nanique uje yoco cucha bisideque iji baruode, mu que
yirajágo gotique gu, mu e yirajágo uje que cucha bisideque ua gotique, yoque
Dupade. Yiraja uje baqueta togode nanique iji taningane, yoque Dupade. Que
uñeque cuse gotique jeta chiqueta totique. Gusu ua uje baqueta togode nanique,
yoque Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, I thank you for helping us to grow in our understanding of your Word. Help us not to forget the things our teachers have taught us about you. Our Dupade, you said, “Whoever is ashamed of me, I will also be ashamed of that person when I return to earth.” This is what you said a long time ago when you were here on earth.

ayoreo:
Eruidaté chujé Ucaré gatoi. Ga
yachẽ́re Eruidaté. Ga yucuase te. Jé yucuase, yumuchase aja te. Mu e dépoji
Ucaré rĩ. Ga yisa pidi. Yisa pidi. Ga yujérase Eruidaté. Mu yajire. Mu
yoquihermáno casidé jõra. Ga yojninga: “E jeodeti
unejángue u ua,” yojninga. “Jé a jé yoquijõra casidé. Yijõra u ua. Uje
yingongo que yuruode aja Dupade iji ti diríca iji iguíjnai ajei,” yojninga.
english: I was in my house. And I saw that Eruidaté was at my house’s door. I thought she came to visit, and to hear Dupade’s words inside my house. I listened to her, but her words were about Ucaré. Everything seemed good to me. And I thought everything would be fine. Despite my preoccupations, I would never have asked my daughters not to come.

ayoreo: “¿O rique?”
english: He sucked it and got full from it. He belched and said, “I didn’t think I’d ever be full again!”

ayoreo: Ore chejna yocugúchade enga ore chijna*Usigáidatéa*ujúbei ore.*Jusédacóde*dóji ore gari ga chumuña. Mu chijíñome ga cho parac enga ore chijnapo rĩ, jocaratade.
english: We arrived back in camp. But when we looked around we saw that they had killed*Jecude*and he was laying over there. They had beheaded him. They had robbed all of our things. They hid my things that they took because they were afraid of our father.

ayoreo: Ore chutai ore ome cojñone jeta ore chujé.
english: They would ask the Totem Pole to give them*cojñone (white people)*to kill.

ayoreo: Chi e iyangóri di. I tu ‘*Carujnángue Erusṍri*’. Chojninga: “Ajoso da ti. Yise gajnerĩ ga yacoté jne.”
english: *Corabé*would say to them: “Hurry up. The person who reaches me first will be my husband right now.” She reached her hand down to help them up. She would say: “Take my hand, take my hand!” But they would get just so far up and then slip down again; they would slip down to the ground.

ayoreo: ***Soríané icatecãraitigade u udojo ome Dupade:**“Dupade Dios, uje yicatecã́ri ua iji de, ipota jetiga yududipise baruode to iji Ecarai, Davidé pijnani jeta chacate yu. Gusu ua uje daijna cuchábi ua ga bajnué cuchade gáyode jnese. Bajnué Satanás, irógode, ore gai. Bajnué pitoringai gai. Yipota jéta acha bajmanai taja yoquibai iji dejai tudé ga yacágoja direcaringuei to gajnerĩ. Acha bajmanai taja yoquibai iji dejai tudé.
english: Let’s*sleep a bit in the sky*and *Soríané will pray for us to understand*Dupade*’s*Words*.

ayoreo: Jé yirasiguipise yájnatigo ore ome carataque-chagúode-daté uaqué.
english: I am so happy about what my grandsons did to that very fierce, man-eating jaguar.

ayoreo:
*Dupade*, átaja yoque iji
gajnerĩ jeta etoque yoque, jé u banguresórone uje ore changuretapise ua.
Yimáco ore iji badode, Yoque*Dupade*.
english: Therefore, I ask you right now for Dugúidé that you take good care of him, and give him health,*Yoqui-Dupade*.

ayoreo: “Angoño yu ga yijína ore que á,” yojninga. Ga ore chacáji ta. Mu cojñone iji ta ga ore chojninga, “¡Boyo!”
english: “Tell me what to do and I’ll go with them,” I said. And they entered the jungle. But the non-Ayoré person said: “Run!”

ayoreo: *Anta*tagu*erámi gatoi, poridie acarane*. Chetingare cuchade mu pogai iji cuchade mu chetingare*poridie acarane*ga que pogája. Doi yodi ga chetingare.
english: Again Dajusui, the tapir, pushed out of his body a type of honey called ‘*jutai.’*

ayoreo:
Yudúco uje chĩ́care purása
quedéjnane. Que yimo jeta ayoréode uyoc u, mu a u uyóguiji erámi jnese uje
que judío uyoque. Mu San Pablo u uje*Dupade*chirógaja ore, uje e changureta Jesus nanique gu, enga cha dasijnaringuéone. Que
chiróguirási gotique uje cho damojo ore, uje chejna ore iji damojoi, mu jé u
uje chiroque Pablo uje changureta Jesuse. Pota gu jeta erámi jnese chudute
dajnacari Jesucrísto ujnomei, uje toi ome ore iji curesei gai.
english: So, Paul and his companions went into the city and met Ananias who took them to his house. He told Paul: “The one I will tell you about is Jesus, the one who saves people. He is the one that made you blind. But Jesus wants to send you to the Gentiles to tell them about him.” This is what he told Paul, and Paul listened to him.

ayoreo:
Yirajágo uje pujopié,
pijnoraquei ore garani tu Dupade, cucha omone garani u Dupade. Yirajágo unojo
uje eduguéjnane, Dupade ujnomei jnungórone ore chingo yoque.
english: The only thing we knew long ago was that there was a*Dupade*, but we knew nothing about his word. We have only learned about the things of*Dupade*because we now have his word. And we now know something our forefathers did not know, that*Dupade*hates sin. He hates disputing and criticizing. He hates disobedience. This*Dupade*wants good things for us, and he wants us to do good things, and he accepts the things we do that please him. **giving****the people****Dupade****’s messages:**

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, bamataté yájnatigo, yatígo ore ome cucha dacadode yúode uje yirãrare ome*Dosadídacóde*iji yejode. Ga yojningame*Dosadídacóde*iji yejode: ‘Jé cachi que ducunire maringa, jé ca yo yinamáji jnacarode ore iji yejode, yujnacarode, yatígo ore iji yirosadode udore.’
english: *Oh, you who caused such awful harm in the past*, you who tried to belittle my grandsons and my sons by threatening their futures. I spoke to*Dosadi’s*grandmother by my side. I said to*Dosadi’s*grandmother by my side: ‘Even though we aren’t*cojñone (*white people), I consider the young men about me as strong as ‘brush fortresses,’ my young sons and older sons for they are all around me, protecting me.’

ayoreo: Darujna – ga ore churúsãre gai; ore chicha cucha pibosode iji údi.
english: For the Totem Pole they used the tall cactus called*Darujna*– they wrote on it; they put food before it.

ayoreo:
A yujóraque ayoré narari jóroque
gajneri: ‘Gayujna todo uati ga que chajnai urúrique go.’
english: I want for*Dijaide*to build a house where we could live alone. Then I wouldn’t have to listen to all the things the women say. They tend to gossip most about those of us women who are believers.

ayoreo: Ujnacari urẽjai chise ta ore idai ujaminóri. E chucué to dacotei mu e chijno uje chirota dacotei. Ga chijno poridie jeta dajei uje ore chaquesu, chaquesu uje ore chucué cuchade.
english: Their mother said: “Hurry up. We must look for your father. Surely that big killer*Dachagaidé*has killed him.”

ayoreo:
Chi Satanas chingo: “¿Jé
batodóningai udore iji deréjnai? ¿Jé unere ome re adie?” chojninga. “Jé
uacamóño anírone cuchade adie sẽrerãja jnumone ga ipotinguerãcho.”
english: But she said: “I’m not going to eat it.*Dupade*told me not to eat it.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: And that’s when the wild pigs started running away from the Ayoreos. They still run from us, because they know what we’re like. And that’s because of what the Ayoré that they took away so long ago told them. Wild pigs know our ways, and that is why, knowing all about us today, they still run away from us.

ayoreo: Enga yajni ga ore chijíñome yu uje yajníngo. Yajníngo. Ducosi tagu yóguiji ta ga yitoi. A yo sẽre mu a yiquecapo rĩ. Enga yicṍraja ta. Ga yijosa yedodie aja jnumi. Yĩriji enga yóji yiyojnai iji ti.
english: I got a fever and I passed out. I probably fainted, because when I came to I was flat on my face on the ground laying in my own blood.

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua uje e baqueta Teresa, yoque Dupade. Bamo uje ejnareté dirica, mu baqueta,
yoque Dupade. Que uñeque cuse ujetiga chiqueta ejnaretátique; gusu ua uje
baqueta yoque. Yacaranguipis ome ua uje udúco baruode iji dirode jnese,
jnanione uyoque.
english: Dupade, help my fellow women and myself to love your Word, our Dupade. We know that there are many of us who do not pay attention to your Word, but I do not know why, because I know that your Word is not something unimportant. Instead, it is powerful. When you were on earth, you even raised the dead with your Word, our Dupade. There is no other person who has raised a dead person, only you can do this.

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai acajnaquéode*
english: Ayoreos – People of the Ayoré tribe

ayoreo:
Yirajágo uje
chi dajéode gare ujetiga ayoréode chajna iji dacuecaringuei tudé. Dupade ũrajémi
sudé ojningai tu uje que toc jetiga ayorérique qué chimesa erámi tuné
jogonónique cucha iji dajei te ga jogadi tu uje cho jetiga ajei doi ñane. Cho
jéta chajna dajeque ajámatique ga, anire, que chimo guesaringárique iji
darosatíque. Ga unojo u uje Dupade ũrajé chi ajãpe gu udojo. Jé basa cojñone
ga ore ũrajei chi cuchape, mu que toc jeta ore chajna jeta ore toi. Que ore
chajna go. Ore guetétigai ome Dupade jé querújna, ga ore chirase raque ga ore
doi ga ore sué Dupade gai ome dajéode, ga ore doi edáyode ga ore sué Dupade gai
ome dajéode. Ore doi dajñaminone, ga jé cachi ore ũrajéreraque dacódejna mu
que toc ujetiga ore chajna udaque jne, dajere uté jetiga ore toi, que ore
chajna go.
english: But I said: “That’s right, for*Dupade*never takes people to heaven,*Ayor**e**o**de*or*Cojñone*without a good reason. Even if you are kind, kind to your fellow men, he wouldn’t take you because of that, for*Dupade*doesn’t save a person just because he’s kind. What he is looking for is whether he has faith that*Dupade*will forgive his anger and his other sins; that’s the reason for*Dupade*to take a person to heaven – his sins must be forgiven.”

ayoreo: Jesus uruode u uje chingo yóguiji*San Juan 17*uje chi idógosi jeta jnome gaté nanique, ga qué catecã́ri dayé iji jnumi ome dacadigode ore. Ga chi chojninga iji dacatecãraitigade: “Yapá, a yijíñome yocurasade ore to iji jnumi, jnutiminórone. Icatecã́ri ua jeta arodipise ore iji jnumi to,” chojninga, “enga arodipise ore ga jé adi tu gajnerĩ uje ugúsi ore gari diríca ga yirodipise ore iji jnumi. Yingo baruode aja ore. Yingo baruode ojningane cucha aja ore, ga jé ore doi jnese. Yimate baroquéode iji jnumi te ga, a yajnésara uaque ga jé ca etaque uñeque.”
english: *Jesús catecã́ri Dayé ome dacadigode Ecarai* **prayed for his disciples when he was about to leave for heaven:** These are Jesus’s words that he tells us about in*John 17*when he would soon be going back to heaven; while he was still on earth he prayed to his Father for his disciples. This is what he prayed: “Father, I’m going to be leaving our countrymen here on earth, these earth dwellers. I ask you to take good care of them here on earth,” he said, “as I have watched over them while I have been here with them. I gave them your Word. And I explained what you said, and they have obeyed everything you told them to do.  I have finished the work you gave me to do here on earth, and now I’m returning to you. Don’t disregard any of them.”

ayoreo:
Mu chojninga:
“Acatecã́ri Yapade ome, jeta araja Yapade,” chojninga. Enga icatecã́ri enga ojninga iji dejai tiji yurigode: “Dupade é, qué icatecã́ri ua iji ne. Yacaranguipise uje babi Jesus chiqueta raja yu, enga catecã́ri yu ome, enga qué icatecã́ri ua ome yu to uje urõsoringuéone uásejna iji yu ujeta átajabá aja yu to,” chiese yojningame Dupade iji yurigode. “Átajape yejnaréngone aja yu,” ojninga iji yurigode ome Dupade. “Yacaranguipise uje babi Jesus chiqueta raja yu,” ojninga.
english: I said: “I know who you are. Who I think you are, dear one, is Jesus, although I’ve never seen you.”

ayoreo:
**Mateo u Ecarai,
Tomása ore abi uje pero iji dabatigai nanique enga que dique to gotique.*
english: *Mateo was Ecarai and Tomasa’s son who was born crippled long ago and he never walked at all.

ayoreo: *(*Ore jno údi ga*Corabé*iguiosode nóna. Abai iguiosode nonapo rĩ. Ore qui tu uacanirone. Que iguínique cus*Corabé*. Jecute que uacaguínique cus. Yimóngo*Juana*(*Dye*) casica ga*Ejenai*chojninga: “A amanique déji sañeque ga aoi póroro to.*Corabé*quic ujáidie gu nanique. A cojñoque póroro irasique yoque uje ore quic ujáidie irasique yoque nanique. Ore quic ujáidie u*Corabé*nanique.” Chiraja nanique*Ejenai*uje póroro.*Queseidáquide, Esoquedéjnanái*chojninga: “Boyo yico. Yajo ore potígo to. Cachi canirac ore iji daquic ujáidie nanique.”)
english: *(**It was probably God blessing Corabé long ago. She was the source of all white people who became enemies of the Ayoreos. The Bolivians who are dark like Ayoreos descended from  the young women named Jongongo (frog), that is because the young men had sex with her without marrying her.)*

ayoreo: Chirote iji dejade, chirote iji dirode uje chi chãraque adie ome cuchade. Ore poté gu. Mu moi chujé gu uje chujé cuchade jnese.
english: He watched over the*‘nujna’*day and night, trying to keep the animals from eating the fruit. That is what the animals came for, to eat the fruit. But fighting off the animals was what he did day and night, and at last he grew very sleepy.

ayoreo: Chi chojninga: “Be yibai ga yijnoque ua.”
english: *Adudui*tried it, and sure enough, it was delicious.*(Samané comments: You know, Violeta, how delicious watermelon is.)**Adudui*ate till he was full. And then he belched and said, “My belly’s full. I’m going to live. I believe my hunger left*¡jo!*I believe I’m going to have food when I need it,” he said. “I feel good! I’m full! I’m alive,” he said again. And then he thanked*Dajusui*.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Manuélné atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger.*

ayoreo: *Adudui*chi chacho erámi. Adi jé u dasocai. Chojninga: “Chaguhei chejna yu jõ.” *Anta*chojninga: “¿O rique?” “Que yĩrarare.” Mu*Anta*chojninga: “Bãrarare, bojninga, ‘chaguhei chejna yu.’ Mu yijnime*cutérone*ome ua.” Chi chaca ga chijnime cutérone. Chajni de agapi. Queru bajo. Chi chojninga: “Apese.”
english: *Adudui*belched then and*Dajusui*said, “Crawl up on my back and I’ll carry you.” And he carried him off into the jungle.

ayoreo: Amoñomi gaté ga yatecã́ri Dupade to. “Yacaranguipise uje Ecarai chacate yogome Dupade Uruode. Yoque Dupade Dios, yacaranguipis ome ua. Yatecã́ri ua ji de. Yacaranguipise ome bajnacari Jesus gajnerĩ uje bagúsi yocari iji de jnumi, Dupade á. Yacaranguipise to uje jé areque yogome ua, Dupade á. Jé areque yoque uje jé barote yoquejode ome pejnaréngone, Yoque Dupade. Ajócãre Satanas utocaidode iji yoquejode. Ga gusu baruode iji yocari iji yoquidai. Yicatecã́ri ua ome bajnacari Jesucristo i. Amẽ.
english: Therefore, having told you this, my teaching is finished, but we know that it isn’t finished. For each Sunday afternoon we meet again to hear*Dupade*’s*W**ord*. Every day, though, is a good time for a person to put his faith in*Dupade*’s*Son Jesus*, if that is what he wants to do. Just let your leaders know and tell them that you want to trust Jesus to save you – do it quickly before he returns.

ayoreo:
Yijnángo Satanase áyipiedie ome
yoque uje áyipie yuque yoc uje yujégo ñane.*Dupade*áyipiedie u uje yijnángo yocate parágode.
english: When we fight with each other we’re fools and are thinking like Satan. When we’re kind, we’re thinking like*Dupade*.

ayoreo:
E yatata diríca iji ucacasé
semána bajei casé uje udute uacajnomei diríca iji de guidai uje chi uacatatayo
uaca-testimónio, ga yirasique uacaruode.
english: I don’t know if any of you, my companions, feel like I do about cigarettes – but I hate them. I’m afraid of people when they get drunk, also. I don’t like them to get near me.

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi*á, jé badi írique durasede uje que asururase paicarupe tiẽchui yirabai uqué durasede, ga qué yugúsiji yebiédatérigui. Ga uñeque datéraque qué yugúcoji yoquebiédatérigui iji uñeque quigáningo.
english: *You who caused such awful harm in the past*, why didn’t you react more quickly, then you could have thrown my grandson down, the one who has the beautiful line-markings as one of his **edopasade*(clan possessions). Then you would have seen me slobbering (Note: another way to refer to crying/lamenting) right now, along with the mother of another victim. We would both be lamenting the absence of our loved ones if you had been quicker – they would have become your victims, instead.

ayoreo: Jé u uje chi datũra changuñéra iyoi aja dabai ga noñáji aoi.
english: And then he said: “Grandmother, I killed*Natingarai*.”

ayoreo:
Jé uacadúcho uje chi qué ocoi
menenéngone, cojñone ore iji erámi uje que ore chudute Dupade Uruode, mu e
yugúcoi ore gatócoro. Jecute Jesuse ojninga casodicaite, gãrapie uñanie e
tocade.
english: You have heard that there are yet many*menenéngone*(people like the*Ayoré*), and other people in the world that haven’t heard*Dupade*’s words, and at one time the*Ayoré*were among them. Therefore, some of the sayings of Jesus concerning the signs of his arrival have already taken place, because now the*Ayoré*have heard.

ayoreo:
Mu jé
ũrépise Dupade Uruode uje que toc jeta Jesuse di enga catecã́ri yóguiji jnumi
tuné; jé educadode déji yocari iji jnumi.*(Hebreos 1:1 aja 2)*
english: *Dupade*’s*Word*is very clear about this, that when Jesus returns, it’s not going to be possible for him to talk with those of us who are left behind; and there’s going to be a lot of tears among us who are left on earth.*(Hebrews 1:1-2)*

ayoreo: Yequésãre. Yipesapo ore dai ejo rĩ, ore dai uñeque, mu tágape yuruode.
english: I’m going to curse someone and make another father for them. But I’m not telling the truth.

ayoreo: Jos eti chicṍri, dayé. Chóji mu areredaté. Mu que chiraja. Jĩrique chipocásupo dacãrai gu. Jos eti chicṍri taja dajoyai. Jé abi gatoi tu udui. Mu cáma chiraja.
english: He also stayed away from the place where they had stored water, that was because*Guébi*had water in his hair; but still, he kept crying. Finally he got nearer to the people despite the campfires the Ayoreos had made, making it too warm for him. The man who had discovered him took hold of his little arm to comfort him.

ayoreo:
Dupade,
Jesuse chojningame yu dirome: “Bacate ore bisideque diríca. Cucha bisideque bacajnaquéode diríca. Que bei déji
darujna aoi to. Que batrabajándoringárique déji yu go. Íjnoque bacajnaquéringo
iji yu rĩ.”
english: If this were how things were with me, then the Lord Jesus would say to me in the future: “You taught people for nothing. Your messages had nothing of value in them. And your name is not found in the ‘book.’ I have not recorded any of the works you claim to have done for me, nor any of your teachings.”

ayoreo: Dupade uruode chojninga jetiga Dupade uruode qui jec u jne. Uacamóño yocaté Eba uje Satanase chãra. Ga chojninga: “Agu, taque Dupade.”
english: But I don’t think Eruidaté had her mind on Dupade. I think Satan was in control of her thoughts. And I didn’t say anything to Eruidaté at all. I didn’t even say, ‘Wait. First, let’s do what Dupade says to do.’

ayoreo:
Mu que yirajágo írique to mu a Dupade
gãranenie u anírenie, cucha adie uje ayoréode yajo to nanique ujetiga
ayorérique tagu ga toi. Chojninga: “Acho jne. Mu ca acho cucha a chojmara uje
déji uté yejnai tudé gatócoro iji ta,” chojninga.
english: After they were made,*Dupade*took them into his garden and said to them: “This is my garden. Eat whatever you want from it. Here’s the*esodie*I planted,” he said. “Here’s the palm fruit called*ijnaque quedéjnanie*,” he said. “Eat some of this fruit from the*picadéode*trees,” he said. “But do not eat this other fruit, I forbid you to eat it.”

ayoreo:
Yojninga: “Señora Marina, jé
areque, yicatecã́ri jne.” Ga e yicatecã́ri.
english: I said: “Señora Marina, that’s right, I’ll talk to him.” And I now pray to Dupade.

ayoreo:
Mu omi tu
jetiga yécãco yocadode uje yicoi Dupade Uruode ga cojñone, Dupade urásarigo, ca
ore chiraja daru quitíguiji yoque. U to ujetiga uerate yocujnoméone. Yécãco
yocadode ga iyagopúsu yocate bajade. Iraja uje yiyágo yocate bajade. Yiyágo uje
jnani bajade chigaru datoidie. Yiyágo uje choquibi yoc. Iyágo uje yujégo
cojñone. Iyágo uje ore chujé dojode ayoré, mu amiri u jeta iyágo udojo jorane
cucha ga yicoi ga yanguretágo Dupade ga yiquihíjnojo aja Dupade gánique jne
jeta di ga di.
english: It is like us Ayoréos who live among*the Cojñone*, we have stopped doing many of the things we did before. We have stopped tying our hair back; we have stopped going about naked; we have stopped killing the*Cojñone*; we have stopped killing our fellow-ayoréos. When we left our old ways, we cut our long hair and become ‘*ones without tails’ (ponytails).*We put on clothing. We carried guns. We ate things that used to be taboo: deer, wild turkey, tapir. We changed in these ways.

ayoreo: Jĩrique yiyapo*Iyai*rĩ. Jĩrique yisapo yujode Etacõre*Jupi*rĩ. *Ijnamene – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: But*Jupi*said: “Go to*Iyai*and sleep with him.” I did, but quickly left*Iyai*also. And I went back to my fellow Étacõre clan member*Jupi*again.

ayoreo: *Dupade*, bagúsi guéode uéchai.*Dupade,*bamo yoque jnese, yoque*Dupade*. Uje ua u, uje bapesute erámi tuné,*Dupade*, bapesute ayoréode ore, enga baraja ayoréode ore yocajéode,*Dupade*.
english: *Dupade*, you dwell above the sky, you see all of us,*Dupade*, you are the one we believe in. It is you who created this earth, you who made the people in it, and you,*Dupade*, know what we are thinking.

ayoreo:
Yirajágo uje
Jesus acajnaquéode a chimate raja jnumi jnese jne. Jecute a ca ayorérique
chojningárasa: “Que yirajágo Dupade atique gotique. Que yirajágo Dupade
ojningane cucha. Jecute que yanguretágo to go.”
english: But*Dupade*would say to that person: “They have my*Word*in Tobité. If they will listen to it and believe it, they will join us here. I don’t refuse anyone from coming here. In fact, I want everyone to come here. It would be wonderful to have everyone here with us.”

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Jonanine, Acui.
english: *Carábiagosode***–**“People of the ‘carabia’.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Tajerogué iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Tajerogue – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo:
E que yotique dépoji erámi jnese
rĩ mu Dupade chi pésu cuchade uné ore. Ujetiga ayoréode yimóngo ipesudode ga
ca yotigáco jec aja dirode unojo.
english: This is not just my words, but they are Dupade’s words for us to read. It says that there was only water long ago. It says that only water was seen on earth. It completely covered the ground, but then Dupade made the water split. And that is how He split the water that covered the earth with the intent of keeping it in one place. All the different water conduits – the rivers, the lakes, the pools, Dupade made water run into them, and they all flowed toward one place. And that is how Dupade made dry land appear. And Dupade finished these things that he did.

ayoreo: Jé uacamóño uje jnani bajai, aníri Ezequías a u, uje chojninga: “Dupade á, a yejnaréngone chinguíra yu aja jnumi dirome, ga a yitoi dirome,” chojninga. “E idógosi jeta yitoi mu, Dupade á, que yipota jetiga acadígui yicaniratiguéode ome yurasade ore,” chojninga. “Jé bamo uje yosõrai yocurása gatadíe ore ome yoquibosode,” chi chojninga. “Yosõraque yocurása matágode ore ome yoquibosode.”
english: But God has heard the news about those who no longer gather to be taught his words, but their news was not pretty to him. The news about them was ugly, for they have abandoned*Dupade*and stopped gathering to hear his words. On the other hand, the news about the people of Tobité, Zapoco and Rincón has also reached*Dupade*’s village, and it was big news for*Dupade*when he heard that news.

ayoreo: Ore cho paraque, uje que ore chiraja daru quitígome yu. Mu quigade enga ore chojninga: “Yimóngo adode ga yirajágo uje emi cuse.”
english: I said to them, “That is right, and we also see what God has done. Look at the trees, the tall trees.  The fact that you live and exist is also evidence of the God who created you.”

ayoreo: Ore erásõre udé corã́cha jnungore adode ga u uje ore cho derásõri iji Chiquenone ore edópasa uqué ga ore edópasa queséode udore iji dati, jé aja guebéode. Ajiinerenie ore. Chiquenone edópasa guebéode udore; ome yocáquiode, ore edópasade jiei. Ore edópasa piguirané. Uje ayoréode ore chimochere gai nanique ga ore edópasa. Chequé bajedie nanique ore chojninga: piguirané. Ore edópasa guebéode rĩ udore iji dati. Ajinerenie jé aja guebéode udore jnese. Picanerane uyoque ga yoquedópasa udaji uacaguíjnane udáji, cochocoidie udaji; cochocoidie udiji ójo. Picanerane ore edópasa udiji ome yocáquiode ore. Ore chojninga: “Picanerane edópasa cochocoidie.” Uje chi Picanerane ore imané ójo nanique ga chi ore edópasa ójo.
english: They say: ‘I’m going to be like the armadillo and carry weapons.’

ayoreo: Enga*Guiéjnané*pojna*Ajópedái*. Chi ore chujérase mu que ore chujé go. Chi ore pojna ñane bisidec.
english: *Ajópedái*was offended and he began defending his sisters-in-law. He scolded*Guiejnanai.*

ayoreo: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia -1960*
english: *Manuelné – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965*

ayoreo:
Mu Jesus
chojninga: “Yiraja. Acatecã́ri Yapade enga atata ore íode rĩ ome Yapade, enga
yiraja ore íode,” chiese chojninga.
english: This is what*Ichajúidé*seemed to say to him.

ayoreo:
Ga unojo u uje chi ayoréode,
cojñone uyóguiji erámi chi yécãco yógaja uné erámi tuné uje yucuegoi
yoquiquetangónique. Mu que erámi tu unojo gotique uje jnani bajade chucué,
udore uje ore chijnóra Dupade. Ore ucuei tu Dupade Idai. Dupade uniri u uje
ore pota chécãre rágaja nanique. Enga Dupade uniri u uje yécãco yógaja to
iji dire uñeque.*(Hebreos 11:9-10)*
english: And that is what*Dupade*’s words, written by Isaiah, tell us. Isaiah is a man who lived long ago. They say he saw*Dupade*and wrote down the things that*Dupade*said. And that’s how he told us about them.  He said: “The people all over the earth, the Ayoreos, and the Cojñone, will make up their minds about going to*Dupade*’s city.” They will say: ‘Come on, let’s go to*Dupade*’s big city!” But they didn’t call it*Dupade*’s city. They called it: ‘*Dupade*’s land.’*(Isaiah 2:3)*

ayoreo: Jecute Pablo chojninga: “Mu uje e uacarajáyo Dupade iji ejode, ga adi jé u uje Dupade chiraja uaque uje chimo uaque. ¿Jé gotique gajnerĩ a? ujetiga ajníñoja uacate bajade. Ore ate bajade u udore uje ore chetaque Dupade iji taningane nanique. Enga ore chetaque Dupade adode. Ga ore gajnui tu uje que ore chiraja cuchárique.
english: Therefore Paul said: “But afterwards, when you came to know*Dupade**,*then the Lord also knew you and looked after you. So, why have you turned back to the way you were in the beginning? Don’t you remember how people are who hate*Dupade?*They hate*Dupade,*and they hate his ways. They are like ignorant people who don’t know anything.

ayoreo: Jé garedie chajni ore gai iji degúi. Ga yico ga yicagoi tõõ. Yiquejnángome jocaratade ga yicagóji ¡tĩĩĩĩ! Mu*Ajópedé*chaca que. Tṍraji te ga yṍrangopísiji. Yijoco jocaratade aja. Jé*Ajópedé*a u. Mu yṍrangopísiji. Ga ore chaquĩ́ra. Ore chojninga,*Ajópedái*chojninga: “A yi ore iji degúi jne. A yingome*Guiéjnané*ore jne.” Uje pojna*Garáijnai*ore diríca.*Garáijnai*aja*Tocoidé*ore. Pojna ore údode diríca. A yibágui ore. Enga a yingome ore jne uje ore dorase yu ga ore chujérase yu. Mu a yipatíora yitique jne.*(*Mi víctima y yo, perezeremos juntos.*)”*
english: Only the children were in the camp. And we kept getting*doridie fruit*and then we heard people calling to our grandpa. And they thought we were there. They kept calling loudly to our grandpa. We were off getting*doridie*. We slept out there. We heard their calls and ran away. We were running and then*Dujuráidacóde*turned toward them. We returned to the fighters in the camp.  **Ajópedatéa*and them were there. But we kept running, all of us.*Ijnerainatéa*, who was an old lady at the time, came with us with*Ojo,*our little sister. And we kept running from the warriors.*Jésedaté, Sigaidaté*were with us.

ayoreo: Uyatágode que ore taguiji erámi. Yoquitódi u jeta ayorérique cho paraque ga chipese dotique cutepec ga chujé ayorérique.
english: The participants don’t eat when entering the jungle. What we fear is that someone will make a mistake and start sucking honey and it will kill someone.

ayoreo:
Enga chi doi daijna gajnaréngone
iji erámi. Chatatame ore mu que ore chiraja urigode go.
english: And so, he brought in all the*daijnane*in the land. He told them about his dreams, but they did not know what they meant.

ayoreo:
Mu beyoi uté gosi ujetiga
chetaque uaque, ga acatecãchoi Dupade ome. Ga jé ãro uté ate gajnarégone ome
uaque gajiné ujetiga yicoi yocangarádejnai Jesuse uru casodicaite ome yoque
ujetiga yicatecãcoi Yocangaráni ome. Enga chijna jne jeta di, enga, ¡socaseti
chijna dirome, ga yitojmárango iji jnumi á!”
english: It’s a good thing to not treat our fellow believers badly, for God protects his believers. They are holy, just as God is holy. But those others, when Jesus comes back again that will be the end of them.

ayoreo: Yijñecárosi iji de. Yisapie ayoré yocuyábi*Pejnócode*iji siñeque. ¿Tangári yu jõ a? Yise Rincón ga yajire cucarani ga yujnusipise*Pejnócode, Pédrode*ore. Dire gajnerĩ ga yajni to gajnerĩ aja Tobité. Cho jeta urugu yu iji jnutígo jeta ca yĩri ga yejna guedosírigo aja Tobité yui. Uneripise guidai yui, Tobité. Chaguhei chejna yu mu yagu ore potígo gajnerĩ. Chi íjnoque jnusiétigade ome. Chise Portón ga unere guidai yui. Jé uneripise guidai yui jeta yagu ayoréode yoquesai*Cucójnai*, ayoréode dacuyábi*Pejnócode*ore posode.
english: I went to find our older brother*Pejnócode*. Maybe he would listen to me. I got to Rincón and whenever I’d look at the mountain it would make me lonesome for*Pejnócode*and*Pédrode*. The next day I took off for Tobité. It was as though, even if I had to go on foot and walk for a month, I would keep going till I got to Tobité. I could hardly wait to get to the village of Tobité. I was hungry along the way, and ate the food that others gave me. And that made me feel better, less lonesome. When the train reached Portón I was excited to reach our town. Oh, how I longed for our town, being there and eating the food that*Cucójnai cooked*and our dear older brother*Pejnócode*.

ayoreo: Ueradipise uaque uje uacangóño Dupade. Uje uacangóño uaquehermánone uacate gajnaréngone. Ga Dupade chojninga: “Angoño uaquehermánone bajaque. Ga quigade ga Dupade e chĩro uacate gajnaréngone. Yocacaranguipise ome udójo, uje Satanáse jiei iji erámi. Mu Dupade uruode chojninga ‘Uacanírique, ca jnoji uacáyipie. Arijínaño uaquejode iji erámi,’ chojninga Dupade.
english: Quickly I said to Eruidaté: “I think you’ve been acting like someone else. I almost hit you. I almost killed you over Ucaré.”

ayoreo:
Ga jé areque gajine ujetiga jé
ca yipéco yocujode Dupade anguresṍre ejóporinguéone to uje Dupade chãraque
danguretaisṍrone gu. Chãjé puyaque ore gu. Puyaque Dupade rĩ. Ga chi e mu
ore cachodi tu aníri, Jesus diringai. “Angureta yu,” chojninga, “Anguretáyo yu
ujetiga jec ũré uaque, ga ujetiga uacojórique ayoré gajnáreque urúrigo ome ua,
ga ca achajnáñoi ore,” chojninga, “uje uacangureni tu yu gu. Anguretáyo yu. Jé
chicha gosi cachodi taja jnumi eruei dirome,” chojninga. “A yajéngome to dirome
ujetiga gosi ca cha date gajnaréngone.”
english: So, let’s not be like the ones who find his teaching unpleasant, let’s be sincere. For when people don’t really believe in God, they can’t tolerate the things of God. They think we’re bawling them out, but it’s Jesus’ words we’ve been telling them. They think we’re scolding them here on earth, because they don’t yet have faith, and they don’t yet have a Savior. They love the things of the world and find them appealing. But God says to you who believe: “Know that the people of earth will speak evil of you, but you don’t have to worry about that. They talk like that, because they don’t believe in me, but in the future, it will work out for your good.” That is what God’s word says. “Those who make trouble for you now, I will make trouble for them in the future when the end comes.”

ayoreo: *(Los nombres están en cursiva)*
english: *Jnani Bajade Anguretigade*

ayoreo: Mu*Dachagaidé*yui tu uje acotei cus.
english: But his sons kept searching, going on trips to try to find him. I don’t know if it was for three months or a year before they found out, because they kept it up for a long time. They say that their mother cried when they returned and told her that the killer*Dachagaidé*had probably killed their father. Their mother cried when they came back and told her that they had come to the conclusion that the assassin*Dachagaidé*had killed their father.

ayoreo: Mu oródi déji ejoi. Yibo uje*Jusé*poji yejode uje edoparé. Yuáre iji yejode. Ga yibo to. Yujnusina yapade enga yibo nanique.*Jusé*po yejode enga yuáre ga yibo to. Chosõre yu.
english: *Umaridaté*announced my arrival and said: “**Jocha*who was lost, there she is!”

ayoreo: Yĩri diríca ga que yiguísãre. A jose ca yujnusíric. Yujnusi mu ayoré boquere yu ga yijñecáraja yajesúode ga cachi que yujnusi. Gapudíe doi yu mu que yijína uñárac. Cachi a yujnusi uñárac, cojñone gachidé. Cachi ore pesapo yujnusiétigade rĩ. Yiya gapu pijõranie, mu yijína uñárac jeta iyajmaque. Yigana iji cojñone ujade udojo. Yáyipie déji jnupepisai mu yujnusina uje que yacotéraque cus ga cachi yáyipie. A yipesapo  yi*cháco*to jne jeta yijínapo uñáraque jne rĩ. Jeta yijínapo cojñone gachidé uñáraque to jne rĩ.
english: I’m singing, but I’m teaching myself as I sing. It’s as though I’m teaching someone else, but I’m really teaching myself. I was lonely, but I stopped being lonely when I started flirting. I was still lonely, but not as lonely. I was playing around, but soon got tired of it. So I’m leaving.

ayoreo: Ore chimate ore ga chetingãre asõrenie uje bajai u Picanerai, ga ore chisi ome Chiquenoi ga chetingãre asõrenie Chiquenoi gai. Dosapei u uje chetingare dasõrenie, ga chetingare Étacõri gai.
english: They finished them and now they gave out lances; first to the*Picanerai*, and then to the*Chiquenoi*. Then they had them try out the lances together. The*Dosapei*tried out the lances, and he had the*Étacori*try them out.

ayoreo: Enga udojo u uje jé yingo yadode aja ore to. Uje jé gusu udojo yabayode uje gare. Que yiyarásu yabai bajai nanique go. A jé ore chiraja to, chequedíe ore, uje pota gapudíe jiedie nanique. Ga yiya to uje potapo uña rĩ. Pota gapudíe jiei. Ga yojninga: ‘A yiya uté to jne uje dopísi Satanás iroquéode gu. Jeta e potapo gapu uegue iji yejoi rĩ ga jé u uje yiya to.’
english: The women said, ‘*Jnani*has a wife but*Sama*wants him,’ but I didn’t want him at that time. But his wife left him and went to Santa Cruz and wanted nothing more to do with her child. It was a long time later, one year or so, that I went to him after his child had died.

ayoreo: “A ca topie yu. Yésariji yodi jne ga ichapo yaoi rĩ,” chojninga.
english: Her girlfriends said to*Corabé:*“Quit playing around with those men who want you. Your skin will get dark. They rub coals from the fire on themselves.”

ayoreo:
Enga Dupade chi chojninga:
“Areque to jetiga jnacari anínique cuse to, atajasónique dojo ayoré ujetiga ore
catecã́ri ñane. Ujetiga ore nóna ñane aja quedéjnane. Ujetiga ore cho uñeque.”
Yingo jnese uje Dupade a pésu cuchisode enga pésu choqui, pésu chequé to
ujetiga anire, cuchisode chõre rac ujetiga quérã ujetiga irágu erámi. Jecute Dupade chisi ome yoque gatei ome dapesudi jnacare, yocai jnani bajai uje i tu Adã. Enga chi Adã chi mo. Jnacare bajai, Dupade imanique bajai, ayoré chi mo. Enga jec u uje Dupade chi chijnime Adã orotabitique. Jnacare bajai orotabidi tenga Dupade chi pésu chequé tiji. Enga jec u uje Dupade chi chimate chequé jeta nóna jnacare bajai enga jec u uje Dupade chi pésu chequé tiji orotabidi. Enga Dupade a chimate chequé tiji, enga jec u uje anire, jnani cho cuyuque to, niji to, iji aniri, damochádi.
english: He was a grown man and his name was Adam. The first young man was made by Dupade from the earth he had made, the same earth where we dwell. All of us humans have descended from the young man that Dupade made first, named Adam. Dupade blew breath into his nostrils and gave him a mind and made him a living soul. Our state of being intelligent human beings started in the beginning and continues to this very day. And from that time Adam was always concerned with all the animals Dupade made.

ayoreo: “Que yĩrarare.”
english: *Adudui*ate and ate. He really liked them. He said, “They’re good. I thought I’d never be full again.”

ayoreo: Tṍra ore ga yote ujósóño u. Ga ore poji yodi. Jé u uje cho jnare ga Guei Basui jno ga nóna aja gaté. Chejna ore. Yirajapídode eruei jé u.
english: When he finished building his house, Falling Rain moved his chosen ones into it. When he got them inside his house, he turned against those on the outside. Those he chose he shoved inside the house.

ayoreo:
Enga chi ore chécãre José
iguidedie. Chi ore doi oite ueradi ga ore chadíja. Tujúna dajúnone to. Chécãre
dadode ga ore chijnímiji eduguéjna iguíjnai uje ore suru gai tiji. Enga chi jno
ga chajnai Faraón yui.
english: They changed Joseph’s clothing. And brought out a new, and beautiful robe for him to put on. He also was given a neckpiece to wear. He was totally changed from how he was in the prison. And he left and went toward Pharaoh’s palace.

ayoreo:
Mu Dupade a
chojningárása ome uté gosi: “Yuruode déji ore gari iji Tobité. A ore chudute ga, ore changureta ga,
ore chicai yoque to. Que yetaque ore to,” chojninga. “Jeta ore íjnogaja
yibai te ga. Ueradipise jeta ore íjnongaja yibai,” chojninga.
english: Ayoréode – People of the Ayoré tribe

ayoreo: Uacamóño uje ayoréode uyoque gare pisagode. A uaque gosi doi Dupade Uruode ome ayoréode ga isai tu ome uje chojmára. Enga uaque gosi sue Dupade anguresõre bajade gai. Uaque gosi sué eduguéjnane, Dupade Uruode jnungórone gai. Chise diri te ga a uaque gosi pésu dejnai. Enga e gare gosi isagode. Jecute jé ũrepíse uje a uaque gosi sue eduguénane ore gai uje uaque gosi u uje uaque gosi que chijnime re ome Dupade Uruode go. Enga chejna, chejna deréjnane. Chejna dirode uje chejna yijmanai. Chise gójni diri te ga changureta rome Dupade Uruode yui. Changureta rome Dupade úringarique irasique yui enga e chajire Dupade Uruode. Uje cha chajire Dupade Uruode, mu e chacate re iji dajei. Pésu dacajnaquéode ome darásade ore ga isagode e gare aja guesi.
english: I remember what Paul said about him: “Tito is not a Jew, so how is it that his faith is greater than ours who are Jews, and he knows more about*Dupade’s Words*.”

ayoreo: *Cojñoquené*– Dosapec – Tiegose
english: –*Picanerãi Clan*and a member of the*Tiegosode*(People of the River)

ayoreo: Enga chi chicháji dajnosode iji ejode. Chisõre iji data. Chi di mu chojninga: “Jé go uje que yimo Dupade ajopític. Que chátaja yu, Dupade.” Mu uné que chiraja gu uje uné jos eti Dupade chátaja ajopídi aja, guejnai aja enga chajo to.Mu Dupade chátaja uyoque gu yojñane. Yirajágo uje Jesuse guẽrate yocucuecaringuei aja yoque. Jecute yirajágo uje Jesuse átapie cuchade aja yoque. Jnani uné gotique chi chojninga nanique: “Que Dupade chátaja yu gotique, yajopídi. Dupade que chajo yejnai gotique to.” Mu uné que chiraja uje Jesuse u átajasṍri. Jesuse ipesute ome, ucuecaringuei. Ipesute ome, etotiguei. Mu ipotigádi upúsu ujetiga cháta guejnatígaja jeta chĩrase. Que chiraja Dupade uruode quigode uñane gu. Jecute chojninga: “Que Dupade chátaja yajopídi aja yu.”
english: If someone doesn’t know this, if he doesn’t realize it’s*Dupade*’s finger that is helping him with his work, it’s as though he doesn’t know anything. When we know that*Dupade*is the one that gives us life, health, and strength, we know this because Jesus has given us this knowledge.

ayoreo: *Dapuré – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960.*
english: So I started thinking about*Ecarai*again, because he didn’t deceive me, as Bariguéjnai had. I thought about*Ecarai*and how he was kind to people, to my older sister that had nothing to eat. I thought about leaving*Ecarai*. And other men told me: “*Ecarai*doesn’t work like I do. Stick with me.” But the men that said these things were just fooling around, and didn’t stay with me.

ayoreo:
Enga adi jec u uje*Dupade Uruode*unojo chingo yoque.
Chojninga: “Ujetiga ca amorase*Dupade
Uruode*iji bajei ujetiga ca jnoji batoi. Cho que ujeta yocajnei mu chise tõ
uñeque.”
english: You know how I’m always telling you about a man that left for heaven. He climbed a mountain. His fellow believers told him, “Don’t go to sleep on the way up.” But he kept going and carried*Dupade’s Word*with him.*Sleep killed him*and, while he was sleeping,*Dupade’s Word*fell out of his hands.”

ayoreo: San Pablo u uje chacate*cojñone*ore. Chatatame udore ore uje chi Jesuse chiqueta raja enga chirógaja daroquéode. Uje chojninga: “A yiroque ua aja purása quedéjnane iji erámi,” chi chojninga Jesus.
english: We Ayoreos were some of the last to receive*Dupade*’s*W**ord**s*in these days. But now we believe in*Dupade*. But we don’t obey his will. I don’t know if I’m telling the truth about us not obeying*Dupade*’s will, but that’s how it seems to me.

ayoreo:
Enga unojo u uje yirajágo
ujetiga apótayo ajína yoque to jnenga, ango yocai Dupade to. Ga Dupade chĩrope
basijnaringuéone. Enga anguretape Dupade uje qué ingome ua aja uje qué eca ua.
Jé basa ga a yitoi jne enga ore déji, Dupade anguresórone, mu ore uruode qué u.
Jé socaseti a toji gosi acajnaquérigo yui.
english: You may know about the men who lived long ago that were called prophets. The prophets were the ones that told about*Dupade*’s words concerning the future. They were sent by*Dupade*, but the people would not listen to their teaching. Those of us who tell*Dupade*’s word today to our fellow people are like those prophets. If you want to go with us when Jesus comes, ask*Dupade*to forgive your sins. If you do, he will put his health in you. He’ll put peace into your hearts. He’ll put love into your lives if you decide to go with us to heaven.

ayoreo:
Enga udojo u uje cáma yicoi to*Dupade*ojningane. Uje chugúperámia
chojninga: “Acho ujetiga aríño, ujetiga e ore chaquesu uaque.” Enga jec ũré
Jesus chojninga to: “Mu acho arurégode enga asáchoja yiríringárique jne ujetiga
amesẽcho yatígo,” chojninga.
english: This is what Jesus told his disciples to do: ‘Drink this juice until I come again,’ he said. He said: ‘Eat this bread until I return, and then I’ll know that you haven’t forgotten me,’ he said. But we haven’t been doing this thing that*Dupade*has told us to do.

ayoreo: Yedobu yujode Étacõre,*Jupi*. Yachopise yujode Étacõre*Jupi*ujosipiedie. Yojninga: “Que*Jupi*chuse guio dajéningo ome Chiquejnone urodie ga chicha yibijnatígoningai ome date ejode. (Canirépise daté mu.)
english: *Ijnamene Chedo Jupi*

ayoreo: (Posode garani tu Dajusui. Adudui jé urugúji erámi.)
english: And then*Dajusui*said, “I’m going to give you some food.” And he passed gas.*(Samané comments: It was like when the food inside of us causes gas and makes our stomachs swell.)*It was explosive and sounded like ‘POO!’  And then, a huge watermelon accompanied the gas as it came out of him. The watermelon was really ripe and red inside. (The blood of*Dajusui*had tinted it red.)  He showed it to*Adudui*and said, “Here, eat this. It’s delicious!”

ayoreo:
Jeta yipótago ga Dupade chisi
ome yoc ‘conciencia.’ Jé unejaque ‘conciencia,’ jec unejaque ‘ciencia’ rĩ. ‘Ciencia”
u cojñone ipesutedatéode uje Dupade chisi ome ore. Dupade urugaite yoc ome
cuchade ujnojo uje chi ipotágo gu. Ujeta yipotágo ga yijnángo. Yocacaranguipise
ome Dupade gajneri uje yuruode garósi mu iji guedé jnoringa tudé mu jé irasique
re Dupade.
english: Dupade is happy when his gifts are not lost. He is very pleased with us when we use his gift of understanding, that he has not lost what he’s given us. Dupade has said to people when he blesses them: “I’m going to give you great understanding.” But it’s called*cognizance*and Dupade gives it to us. But there’s something different, but also a gift that Dupade gives us, that is called*wisdom,*so that we can be*wise*. When Dupade gives us wisdom then we are called*wise people*.*Knowledgeable people*. When we decide to follow God we are wise. Dupade wants all of us to have cognizance – that comes from him. You know that there are people smarter than we are, but they are not better than we are at all. There are things that we can do that they cannot.

ayoreo:
Enga yujnusínango to gajiné iji deréjnane jnese uje Jesuse u uje chopo ore
quique rĩ enga chojningame ore ‘Jesus acadigode.’ Yocacadisṍri u Jesuse to
uje qué cho yoquiquique ome cuchade to iji deréjnane unojo to, uje uté
acadigode jé upo yoque rĩ. Mu adi jec u. Jesucrísto adi jec u uje cho
yoquiquique ome cuchade jeta yirajágo quigode to. Mu arecujaidie u uje chi
chojninga: “Cachi que yiraja ya, gusu Yai uje chiraja. Que yiraja
yiríringárique yúrique mu ujetiga uacapótayo gajiné jne enga a qué yo quique
uñéngome uaque jne,” chi chojninga.
english: We should be so thankful that Jesus brought us to himself even while we were yet sinners. He even had a remedy ready. The remedy was his death.

ayoreo: A jé arajáyo to ujetiga uacacadisóningo ore chacate uágome iji deréjnane. Jeoga uñeque chacate uágome iji deréjnane uñeque. Jé uacamóño to uje anire cojñoque jmatai cuse. Chi cojñoque jmatai isocai u ayoréode eduguéjnarique ujetiga chetaque ayoré noñátique. Ga jé isocai u Dupade uruode enga jé upúsu.
english: When I’m in Tobité I like to pray for you here in Rincón. I pray for the Ayoré all over the world. And the people in Zapocó.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoidé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Cajoidé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*

ayoreo:
Chojninga: “Ucaré, jé yibágui
uaque,” chojninga. Chi, “Yacho uje uacapésu anire, Elena jurédie iji erámone.”
english: **sad****at****é’s words:**

ayoreo: Mu adi jé u dadi uje cajnore uje chijno cutẽ́ri. E chiraja. Cáma chirajapise mu e chachase. “A yajiripise basocai gajnerĩ jeta ajose chicṍri.”
english: At first he didn’t know what the boy was doing. Confident that he was just squeezing water from the plant, he didn’t watch when he was twisting his hair to get water. It sounded like: ¡sooooo, sooooo! When he had enough, the boy invited the man to come down and drink. He said: “Come down, drink what I have squeezed; the gourd bowl is full of water.”

ayoreo: *Ecarai – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Ecarai uruode udojo*
english: But that’s not what*Dupade*’s*Word*says. It says: “If you hear my Word, don’t hesitate. Believe in*Dupade*right then and He will bury your sin.”*(**Hebrews 3:15**)*

ayoreo:
Yetaque Dupade nanique, yetaguipis. Que yo yujodie
chequei to go uje jĩrique ore irasique Dupade. Yetaque nanique, yetaguipis.
english: I was not like the other women at all that so quickly loved Dupade. I hated him, I despised him.

ayoreo:
Yingo yu aja ore to gu yajéode u
jeta yajniapo rĩ. Uje yangári ore ga ore doi Dupade
anguretatodie iquei mu ore tangári ore. U mu que ore cha date gajnaréngone ome yu go.
english: I went before them with the intention of getting right with them again. I listened as others spoke, and how they accepted them, but they still wouldn’t stop their ugly treatment of me.

ayoreo:
Jecute Jesute
chingo yoque iji unojo dacajnaquéode. Chãcara dacajnaquéode aja cucha
pojnánguenie gu uje chi ca jmanéraque cuse ga que charigueta damánique, que
chiro daguidé ueradérac. Enga jé adi u ome chugúperenie uje que jmanéraque cus
enga que toc utacaringome mu jé chise dabosode. Chamáñaje ore ipesungóri u
Dupade uje Dupade u uje chisíome ore posode ome ore. Jecute chacãra
dacajnaquéode aja cuchade unojo.
english: That’s what these teachings of Jesus are all about. That’s why he gave the example of the flowers and said that the flowers didn’t have hands to groom themselves or to make beautiful clothing for themselves. And the example of the birds that don’t have hands and cannot plant crops – but they always have food. That’s because this is how they were created by*Dupade*, and*Dupade*provides for his creation.  Jesus wanted us to know this and that’s why he used these examples in his teachings.

ayoreo:
Jecute gajneri, yicoi ga yijnórango
ñane gajiné. Yoco yoquihermáno iji ñane iji deréjnai tudé. Que taque Jesus go
ome uyoque, mu omi tu ujetiga yanguretágopíse. Omi tu. Jesuse diringai idógosi.
Idógosi Jesuse diringai. Ca yachógo yui. Ca yachógo Jesuse diringai yui.
english: The day is coming when people all over the earth will only be concerned with the things of the world, and no longer seek the things of Dupade. They will not look for a savior. That’s because they love the things of this world. In fact, they’ll make idols out of the things of the world and worship them as though they were Dupade. But for us, it will be a time of great joy when Jesus comes back and finds that we have stopped loving the things of the world.

ayoreo: Enga*Dupade**Uruode*uté u Jesute uje pésu cuchade jnese iji taningai; ga*Dupade**Uruode*jé jogate erámi bajac. Ga*Dupade**Uruode*jé u*Dupade*to. Erámi taningane u unojo uje*Dupade*pésu erámi, enga San Juan chingo yoque, chi: “Cuchade uje déji erámi jnese que ipesute re go.*Dupade**Uruode*ipesute cuchade jnese iji erámi. Jecute yingongo uac uje*Dupade**Uruode*ipesute erámi jogonórone cucha. Que cuchárique cuse ujetiga ca ipesute.*Dupade**Uruode*ipesute cuchade jnese.” *Dupade*chi utocaidi u Daruode (uje Jesute u) uje pésu cuchade. Enga*Dupade*chimate cuchade gu datocaidi iji erámi. Utocaidi u Jesute, dajnacari.*Dupade*chi pésu cuchade enga utocaidi u uje chimate cuchade. Ore chingo yoc uje pucuecaringuei garani tu uté to. Mu ijnangununie, pisijnaringuei pota sué gai mu que toc ujetiga sué*Dupade**Utocaidi*gai uje que sué*Dupade*gai gu. Que cucha uñeque sué*Dupade*gai uje*Dupade*ipesute cuchade jnese enga que cuchárique sué gai.
english: John tells us about when*Dupade*’s*Word*made himself into a man, just like us.*(John 1:14)* And*Dupade*’s*Word*was Jesus, the one who created all things so long ago.*Dupade*’s*Word*existed before earth was made. And*Dupade*’s*Word*was also*Dupade*. In the beginning*Dupade*created the earth, and the book of John tells us about that. He says: “The things that are in the world didn’t just make themselves.*Dupade*’s*Word*made everything in the world. Therefore, we are telling you that everything on earth was made by*Dupade*’s*Word*. There is nothing that he did not make.*Dupade*’s*Word*made it all.”

ayoreo: Mu ãrapatague*Uayé*. Jarie*(mentira)*. Yajníngo uje ãrapatague ome pocaningai iji ta. Chi chojninga: “*Ajópedé*pojna uaque diríca. Ga ore chajni gai*Ajópedái*enga ore chujérase.”
english: But we were about ready to leave and go somewhere else. But they followed us and began to hit us.*Uayé*said: “*Ajocha*’s stomach hurt and they are probably now ‘laying low.’

ayoreo: Adi jec u to uje yijínango Dupade Uruode aja quedéjnane gu jetiga soca seti yacaco uágome Satanás isocade iji pijogadode jnese. Yoquigaidi u ujetiga jé uacamóño uje chi Satanás chi ca chacája cucha gajnaréngone to go. Satanás píode u cucha ueradéjnane ga que ayorérique chiraja gotique.
english: It’s especially like this with us who have good testimonies, those of us who are full of the Holy Spirit, we go everywhere together, and help each other as we serve*Dupade*and preach his*Words**.*We accompany each other and pray for each other when we are sick.

ayoreo: Yoque*Dupade*, yicatecã́ri ua ujetiga átabája uyoque iji deréjnai tuné. Iji deréjnai unojo ujetiga yichago yoquenominone aja ua,*Dupade*. Yoque*Dupade*yirajágo uje pujopié déji ua ujetiga écãre ayoréode yocadode,*Dupade*, ujetiga yimóngo cucha ũréone iji baruode to.
english: *Dupade*, you are ours. We ask you to help us today to turn our eyes upon you. We know how powerful you are,*Dupade*, powerful enough to change us and to show us true things in your Words.

ayoreo:
A ore pésu doidode udé ome cucha
acarane, acarãque ejnúmeone udore. Ujnoro quedéjnanie uje uacamóño ujnoro
quedéjnanie ojodie uje ejnúmecho. Ga ore chugája ñane enga ore pésu doidode
iji.
english: They ate it. But then they said: “We’re naked!”

ayoreo: Jec qué yiyaridirase iji echoi iji ti ore yui. Mu yajire ne mu*Garáijnaidái*casicaite iji ne. Chojninga: “¡Boyo yico to jne a! ¡A yejna yocugúchade iji ti uque que.”
english: But*Jnatua*’s dear father said: “Start thinking of something and we’ll stop farther on and I’ll stay behind you.”

ayoreo:
Pablo cho*pobre*iji jnumi tome darade. Chojninga: “Jé uhúa, ¿gosi te?” Mu chojninga: “Cachi Jesuse tu
yu, bamojoi tu yu,” chojninga. “Uje bo bamojo yu,” chi chojninga. Chi chojninga: “¿Jé yipesutígome
ua,” chojninga, “yangaráni a?” chojninga. Mu Jesuse chojninga: “Bo, acáji
aníri, ore idai. Acáji, ga a ore chingo bapesutique cucha ome ua,” chojninga. Mu yingome uyoque uje yocajnei
jé u to uje jiei jnanione, chequedíe uyoque, mu que yocuñeque changureta*Dupade*iji dáringárique disape to.
